parties: Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. v. CPVO
title: CPVO BoA A001/2022 - Queen
text:
CPVO BOARD OF APPEAL DECISION  DATED 13 DECE MBER 2023  
 
in Case No. A001/2022 regarding  
Community Plant Variety Righ t Title No. 33910 (Variety 
denomination ‘Queen’, Species ‘ Citrus reticulata Blanco ‘) 
 
 
 
 Concerning the appeal lodged by :  
 Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A.      A p p e l l a n t   
Camino del Iryda s/n 03190 Pilar de la Horadada (Alicante)  S p a i n         
  Represented by: Mr Vicente Molés Vilar 
C/ Pascual y Genis num 19 Piso 3 ̊ 
46002 Valencia 
Spain    Against a decision of:  Community Plant Variety Office    CPVO   
3 Boulevard Maréchal Foch, 49000 Angers  France 
 
Represented by: Ms Angela Martínez Lopez, Ms Montserrat García-Moncó Fuente and others     With the Intervention of:  Seedless Gold International S.L.    Intervener 
C/Cabo Salou 19 30720 San Javier (Murcia) Spain  Represented by:  Mr Pedro Bermúdez Belmar  Plaza Alfonso el Magnánimo 12-Bajo  46003 Valencia  Spain  
 
  

     2 
 
On 11 February 2022, Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. (hereina fter: the “Appellant”) lodged with the Registry of the 
CPVO Board of Appeal (hereinafter: the “Registry”) a Notice of Appeal in relation to the registered transfer of the 
Community plant variety right (“PVR”) Titl e No. 33910, variety denomination ‘Queen’ , species  Citrus reticulata 
Blanco (hereinafter: “Queen”) to Seedless Gold Internatio nal S.L. (hereinafter: “SGI”) and related action.  
 The CPVO Board of Appeal, composed of Mr Marcus Navin- Jones as Alternate Chairperso n, Mr Hidde Koenraad as 
the Legally Qualified Member, and Mr Willy Dillen as the Te chnically Qualified Member, gives the following Decision:  
  I. FORM OF ACTION SOUGHT 
 
The Appellant requests the Board of Appeal to, amongst other things:  
 
(i) annul the decision to register the transf er of the PVR for Title No. 33910 concerning 
variety QUEEN (“Queen PVR”) from the Appellant to SGI;  
(ii) declare the transfer of the Queen PVR, includ ing the deletion of the Appellant’s details 
in the register of Community Plant Variety Rights (the Register) regarding the Queen 
PVR, as null and void; and 
(iii) order that the Register is rectified and amende d so as to ensure that the Appellant, not 
SGI, is listed as the titleholder and owner of  the Queen PVR, and the Appellant’s details 
are entered into the Register as the titleholder for the Queen PVR.  
 The CPVO contends, together with th e Intervener, that the appeal is in admissible. In the event the appeal 
is regarded as admissible, the CPVO, together with the Intervener, contend that the appeal is not well 
founded and should be dismissed.    
 II. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS 
 
1. On 21 April 2006, the Appellant submitted an applicat ion to the CPVO for a PVR concerning the species 
Citrus reticulata Blanco  with the proposed variety denomination  ‘Queen’. The application was submitted 
by Mr Renán de Teresa Almenara (“Mr Renán”) acting on behalf of the Appellant. Mr Renán was appointed to represent the Appellant before the CPVO as regard s certain issues pursuant to a form dated 12 April 
2006.   
 2. On 17 December 2012, the CPVO granted the Appellant a PVR for the variety ‘Queen’.   
 
3. On 15 February 2013, the relevant details of the Queen PVR were published in Gazette No. 1/2013. 
 4. On 23 September 2021, Mr Renán sent an email to the CPVO informing the CPVO that there was an 
intention to request the CPVO to register the transf er of the Queen PVR from the Appellant company to 
a third party entity. The email contained a query as  to what documentation should be provided to 
effectuate the registration of the transfer and asking: “
As a registered variety, which model should I send? 
A or B?”. On the same date, i.e. 23 September 2021, the CPVO sent an email to Mr Renán stating ‘ Please 
use option B’.  
 
5. On 24 September 2021, Mr Renán sent an email with an attachment to the CPVO requesting that the 
CPVO register the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI. The email stated that the 
attachment was “ the contract between the companies ”. The attachment was a 3 paged document dated 
‘03/2021’.  
 6. On 25 September 2021, the CPVO sent an email to Mr Renán stating amongst other things: ‘
Further to 
your telephone conversation with my  colleague, […], please find attached a template and a note on 
assignments. To register a new app licant, the Office would need to re ceive a deed of assignment whereby 
the present applicant transfers the CP VRs in question to the new applic ant. Such deed should duly be 
dated and signed by both persons. You may want to use our enclosed template and return it completed 
via MyPVR. ’  
 
7. On 29 September 2021 at 11.08 am, Mr Renán sent an email to the CPVO with a document attached. The 
document attached was a copy of the template document  concerning the registration of transfers, initially 
provided by the CPVO on 25 September 2021, contai ning certain information. The document indicated 
that the request to register the transfer of the Qu een PVR was being made by Mr Renán acting as the 

     3 
 
representative of the transferor entity (namely the Appellant company) and by Mr Renán acting as the 
representative of the transferee entity (namely SGI).   
8. On the same date, i.e. 29 September 2021, the CPVO updated the CPVO Register. More specifically, the 
CPVO updated some of the particulars relating to the Queen PVR in the Register so as to indicate that 
SGI was the rightsholder for the Queen PVR, not the Appellant.  
9. On the same date, i.e. 29 September 2021, the CPVO se nt an invoice to SGI for payment of the official 
fees (€100) for the registered transfer of the Queen PVR.   
10. On 5 October 2021, the CPVO sent an email and lette r to SGI which, amongst other things: (1) confirmed 
receipt of the email from Mr Renán to the CPVO of 29 September 2021; (2) stated that the CPVO had 
registered the transfer of the Queen PVR from the A ppellant to SGI; and (3) stated that the registered 
transfer would be published in the Official Gazette No. 202106 of 15 December 2021.   
11. On 6 October 2021, the CPVO accepted receipt of paym ent from SGI in respect of  the registration of the 
transfer.  
 12. On 7 October 2021, the CPVO’s deputy accountant inform ed Mr Emilio Teresa Almenara (“Mr Teresa”) of 
the Appellant company, that the CPVO had accepted  receipt of payment from SGI in respect of the 
registration of the transfer of the Qu een PVR from the Appellant to SGI.  
 13. On 8 October 2021, the Appellant emailed the CPVO ’s accounting department informing the CPVO, 
amongst other things, that “
…the instructions that [the CPVO] had received to make this transfer, were 
not in any way communicated by [the Appellant company] ”. Several documents were attached to that 
communication including an “ escritura” [deed] stating that Mr Teresa had been appointed CEO of the 
Appellant company as of 24 August 2021. On the same  date, i.e. 8 October 2021, the Appellant sent the 
CPVO a further email with attachments. The email st ated that the Appellant had been the registered 
rightsholder of the Queen PVR as of December 2012,  and had been paying the annual fee to the CPVO 
as of February 2013. It also stated SGI had appeared  to have paid for the annual fee for the Queen PVR 
for the term 2021-2022, but that the annual period fo r the term 2020-2021, which had been paid for by 
the Appellant, expired on 16 December 2021.   
14. On 13 October 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an email informing the Appellant that the CPVO had 
experienced technical difficulties in opening the atta chments to the Appellant’s email of 8 October 2021, 
and requesting the Appellant to re-send the attachme nts if necessary. On the same date, i.e. 13 October 
2021, the Appellant re-sent the relevant attachments to the CPVO.  
 
15. On 15 October 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an em ail informing the Appellant that, at the time the 
CPVO registered the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI the CPVO “
…had not been 
informed of the changes in the board of  directors of the [Appellant company]… ” and that, at that time, 
the CPVO “ had received documents that, acco rding to the data in its regist ry, facilitated the change of 
ownership over the variety in question [from the Appellant to SGI] ”. The email also informed the Appellant 
that, in order to “ re-assign ” the ownership of the variety as recorded in the Register from SGI to the 
Appellant, the Appellant should provide a number of documents including:  
 
“- The attached form through which Seedless Gold Internatio nal S.L. (as owner and assignor) assigns to Angel Teresa 
Hermanos S.A. (as assignee) the ownership of the Community plant variety right on the Queen variety (file no. 
2006/0947). The document must be duly co mpleted and signed by both parties, Mr. Renan de Teresa Almenara in the 
name and on behalf of Seedless Gold International S.L. and Mr. Emilio Teresa Almenara in name and by account of 
Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A. 
- Copy of the deed of termination and appointment of memb ers of the board of directors of the company Angel Teresa 
Hermanos S.A., which you have provided us with  the signatures of the interested parties. 
- An extract from the Mercantile Registry stating the change in the company's administrative body. ” 
 
16. On the same date, i.e. 15 October 2021, the Appellant sent an email to the CPVO stating, amongst other 
things, that the Appellant had never transferred the ownership of the Queen PVR to any third party, and 
that any document which had been provided to the CP VO purporting to agree to the registered transfer 
was “ devoid of total validity ” since it had been “ conceived with a clear criminal intent ” which was to 
misappropriate the Queen PVR illegally and to “ steal it from its legitimate owners ”. The Appellant 
requested the CPVO to provide the Appellant with all the “ forms and contracts ” that the CPVO had been 

     4 
 
provided to effectuate the registration of the tr ansfer. The Appellant also requested the CPVO to 
“immediately suspend the ownership of the QUEEN  variety in favour of the company [SGI] ” including any 
action to give the impression that the owner of the Queen PVR was SGI. 
 
17. On 18 October 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an  email informing the Appellant that the matter was 
“being evaluated by [the CPVO] legal department ”. On the same date, i.e. 18 October 2021, the Appellant 
sent an email to the CPVO repeating its request for the forms and contracts which the CPVO relied on to 
effectuate the registration of the transfer.  
 
18. On 19 September 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an email stating that “ as we have indicated in a 
previous email, the Office has received documents that, according to the data in its records on 29 
September 2021, met the requirements established to  register the assignment of a community plant 
variety right for the variety in question. In particul ar, the documents have been provided by [Mr Renán], 
who is registered as CEO and representative of Ange l Teresa Hermanos S.A. according to the information 
provided to the office ”. At the same time, the CPVO also invited the Appellant to “ take account of Article 
80 of Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 ” and informed the Appellant that the Appellant could “ …oppose the 
change of ownership… ” and “ …submit a request for the deletion of an entry in the registration… ” by 
submitting an application, includ ing relevant documentation supportin g the application. The CPVO noted 
in this regard that the Appellant could su bmit to the CPVO, pursuant to Article 80 of  Regulation (EC) No 
874/2009, documentation such as the documentation Mr Teresa had already provided to the CPVO “ …that 
certifies that on 29 September 2021…[Mr Renán]…was no t authorised to act in the name and on behalf 
of the [Appellant company] ”. The CPVO also informed the Appellant that the Appellant’s access to 
document request would be processed in accordance with Regulation 1049/2001.              
 
19. On 19 October 2021, the Appellant sent the CPVO an  email raising certain issues concerning the 
Appellant’s request for access to documents.  
 
20. On 21 October 2021, the CPVO sent an email to the Appellant informing the A ppellant, amongst other 
things, that the CPVO had responded to the A ppellant’s request for access to documents.  
 
21. On 27 October 2021, the Appellant submitted a request to the CPVO pursuant to Article 80 of Commission 
Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 of 17 September 2009 estab lishing implementing rules for the application 
of Council Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 as regards proc eedings before the Community Plant Variety Office 
(hereinafter “Implementing Rules Regulation” or “IRR” ). That request was for the CPVO to, amongst other 
things, re-register the transfer of  the Queen PVR back to the Appe llant. The request stated, amongst 
other things, that: the original registration of the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI 
was ‘null and void ’ and that Mr Renán had no authorisation to request or effectuate the registration of 
the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI, as Mr Renán did not have legal authority or 
legal capacity to represent or ac t on behalf of the Appellant.  
 
22. On 25 November 2021, the Appellant sent a communicati on to the CPVO informing the CPVO that: (1) on 
3 November 2021, the Appellant had initiated criminal legal proceedings  in Spain against both Mr Renán 
and SGI for falsehood in commercial documents, forg ery in a private document, unfair administration and 
misappropriation; and that (2) in accordance wi th a Spanish court order of 16 November 2021 these 
criminal proceedings were found to be admissible.  
 
23. On 14 December 2021, the CPVO sent an email and lette r to SGI and the Appellant. The letter was entitled 
‘Request for deletion of an entry in th e register – suspension of the case’,  and stated, amongst other 
things: 
‘On 29 September 2021, the Office registered the transfer  of the Community plant variety right for the variety 
‘Queen’ of the species Citrus reticulata Blanco , registered under file No 2006/0947, from Ángel Teresa 
Hermanos S.A. to Seedless Gold International S.L.   The transfer of ownership was registered on the basis of the document provided on 29 September 2021 (model 
ES — option B), signed by Mr Renan de Teresa Almenara, as a person authorised to act in the name and on behalf of both parties, namely the transferor and the transferee, in accordance with the data in the Office’s register.  

     5 
 
On 27 October 2021, the Office received a request for removal from the regist er (hereinafter ‘the application’) 
submitted by Mr Moles Vilar, representing the company Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. (‘the applicant’), on the 
basis of Article 80 of Commission Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 (‘the Rules of Procedure’ or ‘RP’).  
 In his application, the applicant states that the transfer of Community plant variety rights for the variety in question had not been authorised by the transferor, Ánge l Teresa Hermanos S.A., on the ground that, contrary 
to the information communicated and entered in the regi ster of the Office, Mr Renan de Teresa Almenara 
would not have the legal capacity to represent that transferor. 
 
On this basis, the applicant requires the deletion of the registration of the change of ownership, so that it is 
Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A. who is the holder of the Community plant variety right for the variety in question. The applicant also provides documentation in supp ort of his request for removal from the register. 
 On 25 November 2021, Mr Moles Vilar no tified the Office of the lodging of a criminal complaint before the 
Juzgado de Instrucción de Orihuela against Mr Rena n de Teresa Almenara and the company Seedless Gold 
International S.L. The aim of the criminal complaint coincided substantially with the present proceedings for the removal of an entry from the register, in so far as it was intended to verify, inter alia, that Mr Renan de Teresa Almenara lacked legal capacity to represent th e company Ángel Teresa Herman os S.A. at the time of 
submitting the application for a change of ownership for the ‘Queen’ variety. 
 
Since the conclusions of the criminal proceedings before the national courts are strong for the Office to be able to take a decision in accordance with the procedure under Article 80 RP, and considering that the national judicial authorities are in a better position to conduct a thorough investigation of the facts, the Office considers it necessary and hereby orders that those proceedings be stayed on the ba sis of the principles of economy, 
efficiency and effectiveness of administra tive action. 
 The transfer of the Community plant variety right from Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. to Seedless Gold International S.L. is prima facie justified on the basis of the documentation provided to the Office and is therefore governed by a presumption of validity. Conseque ntly, the Office will mainta in the register of Seedless 
Gold International S.L. as owner and th e change of ownership will follow the publication course in the Official 
Gazette.  As soon as the procedures before the national authorities have been completed without delay, the Office will be in a position to take a decision with regard to the request for deletion of an entry in the Register and, in the event that the decision would result in the subseque nt change in ownership of the variety, such a change 
would be duly reflected in the Regist er and published in the Official Gaze tte. Finally, the Office ensures its 
compliance with the principles of administrative and judicial cooperation enshrined in Article 91 of Council 
Regulation No 2100/94, and remains at  the disposal of the Spanish judicial authorities for any request for 
information or documentation they deem necessary.’ 
 
 24. On 15 December 2021, there was a publication in the Gazette concerning the Queen PVR, informing of 
the change in the name of the rightsholder and ot her related details from the Appellant to SGI.  
 
25. On 24 March 2022, the Juzgado de Instrucción No. 2 de Orihuela, Spain sent the CPVO an order, 
instructing ‘
the suspension of the proceedings  for the transfer or transfer of rights of the Queen variety 
which is being carried out by Seedless Gold International S.L’ in the context of the pending criminal 
complaint proceedings against SGI and Mr Renán.   
26. On 20 May 2022, the CPVO sent a response letter to the Juzgado de Instrucción No. 2 de Orihuela in 
Spain, replying to the court’s order received on 24 March 2022. In its letter, the CPVO stated, amongst 
other things, that: (1) the CPVO had included, in the Register, informatio n on the existence of the pending 
criminal proceedings in Spain; and that (2) the CP VO was not in a position to carry out the interim 
suspension as requested by the Spanish court, since,  according to the CPVO, the procedure of registering 
the transfer of the Queen PVR was not a pending issue,  as was implied in the order, but an issue which 
had already been effectuated and concluded.  
27. On 15 June 2022, details of the Spanish criminal proceedings were published in the Gazette 3/2022, 
stating ‘
Court of Instruction n ◦2 - Orihuela – ES Preliminary proceedings - N ◦002286/2021 ’ and ‘ Offence 
of forgery of a commercial and private docume nt, unfair administration and misappropriation ’. 
 
III. PROCEDURE BEFORE THE BOARD OF APPEAL 
 
28. On 11 February 2022, the Appellant lodged with the Regi stry, a Notice of Appeal concerning the registered 
transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI.   

     6 
 
29. On 2 March 2022, SGI informed the Registry that it  wished to participate in the appeal proceedings 
concerning Case A001/2022 (Queen).   
 
30. On 31 March 2022, the appeal was remitted to the Board of Appeal. 
 
31. On 13 April 2022, the Appellant submitted to the Registry its Grounds of Appeal. 
 
32. On 15 April 2022, details of Board of Appeal Ca se A001/2022 (Queen) were published in the Gazette 
2/2022, stating ‘ Request to delete information in the Register (change of holder) ’. 
 
33. On 10 June 2022, SGI submitted to the Registry its fi rst observations on the Appellant’s Notice of Appeal 
and Grounds of Appeal.  
 
34. On 15 June 2022, the CPVO submitted to the Registry its first observations on the Appellant’s Notice of 
Appeal and Grounds of Appeal. 
 
35. On 3 August 2022, the Appellant submitted its reply ob servations to the Registry responding to the first 
observations of both the CPVO and SGI.  
 
36. On 11 October 2022, the Board of Appeal invited the CP VO and SGI to: (1) provide rejoinder observations 
in response to the Appellant’s reply observations da ted 3 August 2022; and (2) to respond to the questions 
from the CPVO Board of Appeal addressed to them  respectively, and provide any other relevant and 
related observations concerning these questions.  
 
37. On 14 October 2022, the CPVO requested a time ex tension of 4 months to submit its rejoinder 
observations, and answers to the qu estions of the Board of Appeal. 
 
38. On 20 October 2022, the Board of Appeal granted the re quested time extension and informed the parties.  
 
39. On 13 March 2023, SGI submitted its rejoinder observat ions and written answers to the Registry of the 
CPVO Board of Appeal. 
 
40. On 14 March 2023, CPVO submitted its rejoinder observ ations and written answers to the Registry of the 
CPVO Board of Appeal. 
 
41. On 13 June 2023, the Appellant provided written ob servations on the CPVO and SGI responses to the 
Board of Appeal questions. The Appellant also inform ed the Board of Appeal that it did not request an 
oral hearing.  
42. On 15 June 2023, the CPVO requested an oral hearing. 
 
43. On 8 August 2023, the CPVO Board of Appeal served on the parties a summons to attend an oral hearing.  
 
44. On 20 September 2023, the oral hearing was held, follo wing which the oral proceedings were closed.   
  IV. ADMISSIBILITY  
 45. The CPVO asserts, together with the Intervener, that the appeal is inadmissible  for, amongst others, the 
following reasons.   
46. First, according to the CPVO, the Appellant has limited its appeal to the ‘
decision dated 14 December 
2021’ (“14 December 2021 Act”). According to the CPVO, in doing so, the Appellant has thereby limited 
and restricted the subject matter of this appeal to the 14 December 2021 Act. At the same time, and 
according to the CPVO, the 14 December 2021 Act is not a measure against which an appeal may be brought before the CPVO Board of Appeal. The CPVO  argues that the 14 December 2021 Act does not 
constitute a final decision of the Office, but a non- appealable administrative act of procedure. More 
specifically, the CPVO states that the purpose of the 14 December 2021 Act was to suspend and stay the 
proceedings initiated pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation. The CPVO takes the 
view that, as the 14 December 2021 Act does not br ing an end to the procedure for removing the 

     7 
 
registration of the transfer of the Queen PVR, th e 14 December 2021 Act is not a measure against which 
an appeal may be brought before the CPVO Board of Appeal and therefore must be regarded as 
inadmissible. The CPVO further argues that, should th e appeal be considered admissible, this would imply 
that the Board of Appeal would have the task of carr ying out an assessment of facts and of law which the 
CPVO has not yet been able to carry out in the fi rst instance. This, according to the CPVO, would be 
contrary to the principles of procedural la w as laid down in European case law.  
 
47. Second, according to the CPVO, the Appellant ha s not appealed, directly or indirectly, the “ Office’s 
communication of 5 October 2021 ”. That is because, according to the CPVO, it may be inferred that the 
Appellant cites the ‘change of ownership agreement’ as  a substantive element addressed in that decision, 
and the ‘publishing’ of the agreement as a consequenc e of the effects of that ‘decision’. This means, 
according to the CPVO, that the Appellant does not dire ctly or indirectly appeal the Office’s communication 
of 5 October 2021.   
48. Third, according to the CPVO, the A ppellant cannot appeal against the pu blication in the Official Gazette 
of 15 December 2021 as this does not constitute an  appealable decision under Article 67 of Council 
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 (“Basic Regulation”).  
49. The Appellant contests the arguments advanced by the CPVO and asserts the appeal is admissible.  
 
Findings of the Board of Appeal 
 
50. Before assessing whether the conditions of admissibi lity are met in this appeal case, the Board of Appeal 
finds it necessary, as a preliminary point, to address the issue of proceedings initiated pursuant to Article 
80 of the Implementing Rules Regula tion, and the relevance of those proceedings vis-à-vis the appeal 
brought by the Appellant before this Board of Appeal  – namely appeal case A001/2022. In this regard the 
Board of Appeal notes the following points. 
 51.  Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation states:    
Article 80  
Conditions for entries in the Registers  
Without prejudice to other provisi ons of the basic Regulation or of  this Regulation, a request for 
an entry or a deletion of an entry  in the Registers may be made by any interested person. The 
request shall be made in writing, accompanied by supporting documents. 
 
52. Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation is  housed within the Implementing Rules Regulation. 
That Regulation, as stated in its title, establishe s implementing rules for the application of Council 
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 as regards proceedings be fore the Community Plant Variety Office. It is 
settled case law that an implementing regulation must , if possible, be given an interpretation consistent 
with the basic regulation (See,  for example, Case C-32/00P 
Commission v Boehringer Ingelheim 
Vetmedica GmbH and Another, ECLI:EU:C:2002:119,  [2002] ECR I-1917, paragraph 53 and the case-law 
cited therein) and that interpretation of EU law should, in prin ciple, not negate the rights of an individual 
(see, for example, Case C-340/97 Nazli and Others v Stadt Nürnberg, ECLI:EU:C:2000:77, [2000] ECR I-
957, paragraph 35). It therefore follows that the ru les and requirements set out in the Implementing 
Rules Regulation should be interpreted consistently an d in line with the rules an d requirements set out in 
the primary legislation – namely the Basic Regulation, and should not be interpreted in such a way as to 
negate the rights afforded to partie s pursuant to the Basic Regulation.  
 53. Moreover, Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regu lation expressly states that that provision applies 
“
without prejudice to other provis ions of the basic Regulation… ”. Article 80 of the Implementing Rules 
Regulation cannot, therefore, be regarded as altering the scope or application of the provisions set out in 
the Basic Regulation as that would have the effect  of upsetting the balance that the legislature had 
intended to achieve when adopting the Implementing  Rules Regulation. The EU legislature alone has the 
competence to strike that balance (See the Opinio n of the Advocate General of 2 April 2020, Case 
C-264/19, Constantin Film Verleih GmbH v YouTube LLC, Google Inc. , ECLI:EU:C:2020:261, paragraphs 
57-58 and the case-law cited therein).   
 54. It follows from the above, that Article 80 of th e Implementing Rules Regulation therefore cannot be 
interpreted as calling into question the legal rights afforded under the Basic Regulation, in cluding the right 
to bring appeals before this Board of Appeal. This wo uld not be in line with the legislature’s intention – 
as explicitly expressed in Article 80 of the Implementi ng Rules Regulation itself. Moreover, to do so may 
negate or prevent parties from exercising their rights  to bring appeals before the CPVO Board of Appeal 

     8 
 
– a right that is codified and set out in the Basic Regu lation. As such, in those si tuations where proceedings 
pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation have commenced, and where the CPVO has 
decided to suspend/stay those proceedings – as is the situation here – neither the initiation of proceedings 
pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rule s Regulation, nor the su spension/stay of those 
proceedings, can serve, per se, to negate, prevent or pr eclude parties from exercising their legal right to 
bring an appeal before this Board of Appeal in accordance with Chapter V provisions of the Basic 
Regulation, and related law. It follo ws that the CPVO may take action in the context of the Article 80 of 
the Implementing Rules Regulation, bu t that action cannot serve to negate or deny the Appellant’s right 
to bring an appeal before this Board of Appeal, or to  prevent this Board of Appeal from hearing the appeal 
or taking relevant action in relation to it.    
 
55. The Board of Appeal further notes, in this regard, that the appeal procedure provided for in Chapter V of 
the Basic Regulation – and which concerns this Boar d of Appeal on the one hand, and the ability for 
natural and legal persons to submit requests pursuant  to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation 
on the other – cannot be regarded as the same or as  serving the same legal purpose. Article 80 of the 
Implementing Rules Regulation affords relevant persons the ability to submit a request. In this case the 
Appellant has submitted a request, pursuant to Arti cle 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, for the 
particulars in the Register concerning the Queen PVR to  be changed so as to delete the name and details 
of the currently listed rightsholder  (namely SGI), and to insert the Ap pellant’s name and relevant details. 
At the same time the Appellant has lodged an appeal befo re this Board of Appeal to annul the initial action 
taken by the CPVO to change the particulars in the Register concerning the Queen PVR by deleting the 
Appellant’s name and relevant details as the rightsho lder, and inserting SGI’s name and relevant details. 
It therefore follows that although the Appellant seeks to achieve a si milar outcome via both proceedings, 
legally the procedures pursuant to Article 80 of the Im plementing Rules Regulation and pursuant to Article 
67 of the Basic Regulation, are distinct and different  and not the same. That is because, amongst other 
things, by the Appellant’s request submitted pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, 
the Appellant is requesting the CPVO to take certain ac tion in the future regardin g the entry in the Register 
concerning the Queen PVR.  Whereas, in contrast, by lodging an appeal before this Board of Appeal, the 
Appellant is seeking, amongst other things, the annulment of an act taken by the CPVO in the past. As, 
amongst other things, these administrative procedur es must be regarded as separate and distinct, 
contrary to the submissions from the CPVO, the Board of Appeal finds action or inaction taken by the 
CPVO pursuant Article 80 of the IRR cannot be regarded  as having a direct and/or automatic bearing on 
appeal procedures before this Board of Appeal.  
 56. As regards to whether the Board of Appeal should stay the appeal proceedings, in a similar way as the 
CPVO has suspended the processing of the Appellant’ s Article 80 IRR request, the Board of Appeal notes 
that it is for the Board of Appeal, in its discretion, to decide whether or not to stay appeal proceedings. 
Relevant parties may lodge requests and applications to the Board of Appeal for appeal proceedings to 
be stayed. However, merely because a party has subm itted a request pursuant to Article 80 of the IRR 
and the CPVO has suspended the assessment of that re quest – does not affect a party’s right to bring 
appeal cases before this Board of Appeal, or for this  Board of Appeal to hear the appeal case. In any 
event, the Board of Appeal notes that it has not receiv ed a request or application from the parties in this 
appeal case to stay the appeal proceedings, and the Board of Appeal has not deemed it necessary, of its 
own initiative, to stay the appeal proceedings.  
 57. The Board of Appeal also notes that, as the CPVO has correctly pointed out during the written proceedings 
and at the oral hearing, the publication of informatio n in the Gazette is not a mere procedural formality, 
but instead plays an important role in EU PVR legal sy stem, not least as it can set the date by which time 
appeals must be lodged before this Board of Appeal or the courts. It therefore follows that, decisions 
adopted by the CPVO should, in principle, be publishe d in the Gazette before or at the same time, as the 
acts or measures prescribed within them in accordan ce with the principle of good administration, legal 
certainty and legitimate expectation. Where this is not the case, it may result in the initiation of different 
CPVO procedures at the same time – including, as is the case here – appeal proceedings before this Board 
of Appeal and requests submitted pursuant to Article 80 of the IRR. For the avoidance of doubt, the Board 
of Appeal takes the view that parties must be afforded  their legal right to bring appeals before this Board 
of Appeal which have suspensory effect pursuant to Article 67(3) of the Basic Regulation – and acts or omissions by the CPVO should not call th is legal right into question.      
 58. The Board of Appeal therefore concludes that mere ly because proceedings pursuant to Article 80 of the 
Implementing Rules Regulation have  been initiated and/or suspended/stayed, that cannot and does not 
affect a party’s legal rights to bring an appeal case  before this Board of Appeal. As such the Board of 
Appeal concludes that proceedings initiated or susp ended pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing 

     9 
 
Rules Regulation cannot and do not, per se, render  proceedings before this Board of Appeal as 
inadmissible, and, in any even t, does not render appeal case A001/2022 as inadmissible. 
 59. Having addressed the preliminary issue of admissibi lity and proceedings initia ted or suspended pursuant 
to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, it  is necessary for the Board of Appeal to address 
whether or not the conditions of admissibility, as la id down in the Basic Regu lation, the Implementing 
Rules Regulation, and other relevant  law - are met in this case.  
  60. As regards to the general subject matter and admissibili ty, the Board of Appeal notes the following points.  
 
61. This appeal case concerns, amongst other things, the deletion and entering of information on the Register 
concerning the Queen PVR, in particular the name and related details of the rightsholder. 
 62. In this regard, Article 67 of the Basic Re gulation states, amongst other things, that “An appeal shall lie 
from decisions of the Office which have been taken pu rsuant to […], as well as on decisions related to 
[…] the entering or deletion of information in the Register pursuant to Article 87…”  (Article 67(1) of the 
Basic Regulation).  
 63. Article 87 of the Basic Regulation states, amongst other things, that “ The Office shall keep a Register of 
Community Plant Variety Rights wherein, after grant of a Community plant variety right, the following 
particulars shall be entered: […] (d) the name and address of the holder, of  the breeder and of any 
procedural representative concerned ” (Article 87(2) of the Basic Regulation).  
 
64. It is not contested by the parties in this appeal case that the CPVO has, in fact and in law, adopted 
measures to delete some of the details contained in the Register concerning the particulars for the Queen 
PVR and entered other information in the Register co ncerning the particulars for the Queen PVR. More 
specifically, it is not contested by the parties that  the CPVO has adopted measures to delete the name 
and relevant details of the Appellant as the rightshold er and enter SGI’s details as the rightsholder for the 
Queen PVR. As expressly stated in Article 67 of the Basi c Regulation, an appeal lies before this Board of 
Appeal regarding the entering and deletion of ce rtain titleholder information in the Register.   
 65. Given the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that  the general subject matter falls within the scope of 
measures which are subject to appeal before the CPVO Board of Appeal – namely, and amongst other 
things, the deletion and entering of informatio n on the Register concerning the Queen PVR.  
 66. As regards to the specific acts and measures being challenged by the Appellant, and whether or not those 
acts are subject to appeal before the CPVO Board of  Appeal, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO 
essentially contests that although the Appellant ha s raised an appeal against the 14 December 2021 Act, 
that act, according to the CPVO, is not subject to a ppeal for two reasons. Firstl y, according to the CPVO, 
the CPVO never intended that the 14 December 2021 Act to be appealable before the CPVO Board of Appeal and, instead intended the 14 December 2021 Ac t merely to be a communication relating to the 
proceedings initiated under Article 80 of the Implemen ting Rules Regulation. Se condly, according to the 
CPVO, the 14 December 2021 Act is not, and was not in tended to be, according to the CPVO, a final act 
but merely a communication informing the Appellant of the suspension/stay of a procedure (namely the 
suspension/stay of the procedure under Article 80 of  the Implementing Rules Regulation) which has not 
been concluded or finalised.  
 67. Before assessing the merits of these arguments, the Board of Appeal recalls, as a preliminary matter, the 
following points. 
 68. It is settled case-law that whether an act adopted by  an EU institution or agency is open to appeal or 
challenge, depends upon whether that act or measure, whatever its form, is intended to have binding legal effects. If so, that act or measure is, in princi ple, open to appeal or challenge (see, for example, 
Judgment of the General Court of 8 May 2018, 
Esso Raffinage, v European Chemicals Agency (ECHA) , 
Case T–283/15, ECLI:EU:T:2018:263, paragraphs 49; judgments of 31 March 
1971, Commission v Council , 22/70, EU:C:1971:32, paragraph 42; of 2 March 
1994, Parliament v Council , C-316/91, EU:C:1994:76, paragraph 8; and of 13 October 2011, Deutsche 
Post and Germany v Commission , C-463/10 P and C-475/10 P, EU:C:2011:656, paragraph 36). If, on the 
other hand, an act or measure does not produce binding legal effects, such as preparatory acts, confirmatory measures and implementing measures, mere recommendations and opinions and, in principle, internal instructions – then those acts an d measures generally fall outside the scope of review 
by the EU Courts and Boards of Appeal (see, for example, Judgment of the General Court of 8 May 2018, 
Esso Raffinage, v European Chemicals Agency (ECHA) , Case T–283/15, ECLI:EU:T:2018:263, paragraphs 

     10 
 
50, order of 14 May 2012, Sepracor Pharmaceuticals (Ireland) v Commission , C-477/11 P, not published, 
EU:C:2012:292, paragraph 52; see also, to that  effect, the judgment of 12 September 2006, Reynolds 
Tobacco and Others v Commission , C-131/03 P, EU:C:2006:541, paragr aph 55 and the case-law cited). 
The Board of Appeal further notes that Article 67 of th e Basic Regulation provides that: “An appeal against 
a decision which does not terminate proceedings as regards one of the parties may only be made in 
conjunction with an appeal against the final decision , unless the decision provides for separate appeal” 
(Article 67(4) of the Basic Regulation).  
 69. According to settled case-law: 
The capacity of a measure to produce binding le gal effects and, therefore, be open to challenge 
[…] must be assessed in accordan ce with objective criteria, su ch as the contents of that 
measure, taking into account, as appropriate, t he context in which it wa s adopted and the powers 
of the institution which adopted the m easure (see, judgment of 13 February 
2014, Hungary  v Commission , C-31/13 P, EU:C:2014:70, paragr aph 55 and the case-law cited). 
The assessment of the content of a contested act consists in ex amining its substance (judgment 
of 11 November 1981, IBM v Commission , 60/81, EU:C:1981:264, paragraph 9), whilst also 
taking into account its wording (see, to that effect, the judgment of 20 March 
1997, France  v Commission , C-57/95, EU:C:1997:164, paragraphs 9 to 23). It is possible to 
have regard also to subjective criteria, such as the intention of the author of the act in question 
(see, to that effect, the judgments of 17 July 2008, Athinaïki Techniki  v Commission , C-521/06 P, 
EU:C:2008:422, paragraph 42,  and of 26 January 2010, Internationaler 
Hilfsfonds  v Commission , C-362/08 P, EU:C:2010:40, pa ragraph 52). (see, for example, 
Judgment of the General  Court of 8 May 2018, Esso Raffinage, v European Chemicals Agency 
(ECHA) , Case T–283/15, ECLI:EU:T:2018:263, paragraphs 51).  
70. In this case, the 14 December 2021 Act itself states amongst other things that  
“On 29 September 2021, the Office  registered the transfer of the Community plant variety right 
for the variety ‘Queen’ of the species Citrus reticu lata Blanco, registered under file No 2006/0947, 
from Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. to  Seedless Gold International S.L. ” (emphasis added) 
71. As stated at paragraphs [7-8] above, on the 29 September 2021, the CPVO updated the CPVO Register, 
in particular the CPVO updated some of the particular s relating to the Queen PVR on the Register so as 
to indicate that SGI was the rightsholder  for the Queen PVR, not the Appellant.  
72. The Board of Appeal therefore notes that on 29 September 2021 the CPVO did not adopt a mere initial 
opinion or provisional view as regards the deletion and entering of details on the Register concerning the Queen PVR. Instead, on 29 September 2021 the CPVO ad opted a decision to delete and enter details on 
the Register. Moreover, having adopted that decision , on 29 September 2021, the CPVO actually did, in 
practice, take the relevant action - and deleted an d entered the relevant details on the Register. It 
therefore follows that the CPVO’s decision of 29 September 2021 was intended to be final and have 
binding legal effects – and did have such effects in fact and law. 
73. Moreover, it is clear from the CPVO’s submissions in  this appeal case, that th e CPVO does not regard the 
deletion of the Appellant’s name and details from the Register as regards the Queen PVR, or the entering 
of SGI’s name and details in the Register as regards the Queen PVR, as being a mere opinion or provisional 
view. On the contrary, as the CPVO pointed out in its oral submissions during the oral hearing, and as 
reflected in its comments to the Spanish Court as outl ined at paragraph [26] above – in the CPVO’s view 
– it is not possible to suspend the act concerning th e change of the Register to indicate SGI as the 
titleholder for the Queen PVR, as that act has already occurred.  According to th e CPVO, the only question 
remaining is whether the particulars of the Queen PVR should, in the fu ture, be changed again so as to 
delete SGI’s details and insert thos e with the Appellants. It therefor e follows that the CPVO’s act and 
measure of resolving to amend the particulars of the Queen PVR so as to delete the Appellant’s name and 
details, and replace those with SGI’s – must be regarded as a final act.  
74. As regards to the CPVO’s contention that the CP VO did not intend for the 14 December 2021 Act to be 
an act or measure open to challenge, the Board of A ppeal note that although the subjective intention of 
an institution or agency is of rele vance in assessing whether, in fact and law, the act itself is open to 
challenge – subjective intention is not the sole or ex clusive factor that must be taken into account in 
assessing whether the act or measure in question is op en to challenge. Instead, regard must be had to, 

     11 
 
amongst other things, the obje ctive factors (see, for example, Judgment of 21 January 2021, Federal 
Republic of Germany v Esso Raffinage , Case C-471/18 P, ECLI:EU:C:2021:48, paragraph 65 – and the 
caselaw cited therein).  
75. In this regard, the Board of Appeal notes that, in th is case, as expressly and explicitly stated in the 14 
December 2021 Act itself “On 29 September 2021, the Office  registered the transfer”. The Board of Appeal 
notes that the act of ‘registering a transfer’ involves, by its very nature, the deletion of certain information 
in the particulars of the Queen PVR on the Register , and the entering of other information in those 
particulars on the Register. i.e. the act of registering a transfer by its very nature constitutes a decision 
to delete certain information, and enter other informatio n, in the particulars of a PVR in the Register. This 
is the case in particular where th e deletion and/or entering of information on the Register cannot be 
regarded as merely an editorial change, or an am endment of a minor or self-evident mistake via a 
corrigenda or other similar act.   
76. The Board of Appeal further notes that acts or meas ures concerning the deletion of information contained 
in the particulars for a PVR on the Register legally cons titute, as expressly stated in Article 67 of the Basic 
Regulation, “ decisions ” by the CPVO, which are subject to appeal  before this Board of Appeal. As such, 
having regard to the legal context within which the measure was adopted in this case, more specifically 
the CPVO’s act of determining to “ register the transfer ”, the Board of Appeal further finds that, according 
to the relevant objective criteria, that the CPVO’s de cision to delete and enter certain information on the 
Register produced binding legal effects and is , therefore, an act subject to challenge.  
77. It follows from the above, that  the CPVO’s decision to delete an d enter certain information from the 
particulars on the Register concerning the Queen PVR,  is an act open to appeal before this Board of 
Appeal and, as such, is admissible in this regard.  
 
78. Having assessed the relevant conditions of admi ssibility concerning the general subject matter and the 
specific acts being contested, it is necessary for th e Board of Appeal to determine whether the Appellant 
is a party entitled to bring an appeal before this Board of Appeal. In this regard, the Board of Appeal 
notes that, pursuant to Article 68 of the Basic Regula tion, only those natural or legal persons to whom a 
decision is addressed, or who are directly and indivi dually concerned by it, are entitled to bring appeals 
before this Board of Appeal. In interpreting the concep t of ‘direct and individual concern’ in Article 68 of 
the Basic Regulation, the Board of A ppeal must be guided by the settled case law of the Court of Justice 
of the European Union.  
 79. For a measure to be subject to an appeal or action for annulment, it is settled case-law that the measure 
does not have to be formally entitl ed a decision – but must, instead, ch ange the rights of the person to 
whom it is addressed (see, Joined Cases 8-11/66 
Cimenteries CBR Cementbedrijven NV v EEC Commission  
[1967] ECR pages 75-91 [1967] CMLR, paragraph 103). 
 80. As the Board of Appeal has already stated at para graphs [67-77] above, by resolving to register the 
transfer of the Queen PVR right from the Appellant to SGI and ther eby deleting and entering the relevant 
details in the particulars stated on the Register fo r the Queen PVR, the CPVO adopted a decision. That 
decision was first formally communi cated by the CPVO to the Appellant on 14 December 2021 by way of 
a letter. That letter was sent by the CPVO to SGI and,  in addition, the Appellant. This is indicated in the 
letter itself which states, amongst other things, “
Sent by registered mail and email to: Angel Teresa 
Hermanos S.A. (CPVO Client No 05449) refresh. NG/20210151029 ” and which contains the full name and 
address of the Appellant within that document. It therefore follows that the 14 December 2021 Act itself 
was addressed to the Appellant. It  also follows that the decision taken by the CPVO on 29 September 
2021 and concerning the deletion and entering of ce rtain information on the Register concerning the 
Queen PVR – and which the Appellant was informed of  in the 14 December letter – was also addressed 
to the Appellant. It follows that the Appellant is an addressee of the decision as  provided for in Article 68 
of the Basic Regulation.    
 
81. In any event, the Board of Appeal notes that, purs uant to Article 68 of the Ba sic Regulation, natural or 
legal persons who are not addressees, but who are dire ctly and individually concerned by a decision, are 
also entitled to bring appeals before this Board of Appeal.  
 82. In this regard, and as concerns direct concern, it is settled case law that for an act to be of direct concern 
to a natural or legal person it must directly affect th e legal situation of an appellant and leave no discretion 
to the authorities responsible for implementing that act, such implementation being purely automatic and 
resulting from EU law alone, without the applicatio n of other intermediate rules (See Judgment of the 

     12 
 
European Court of Justice, 22 June 2021, Bolivarian Republic of Venezu ela v Council of the European 
Union , Case C-872/19 P, ECLI:EU:C:2021:507, paragrap h 61 and the case-law cited therein).  
 83. In this case, the decision on 29 September 2021 to delete and enter information on the Register 
concerning the Queen PVR to indica te the change of the rightsholder , the subsequent communication and 
publication of that decision, and the change to the Re gister itself, impeded the Appellant’s legal ability to 
assert itself as the legitimate legal owner of the PVR,  and claim the legal, financial and other benefits of 
possession and ownership of the PVR itself – including, for example, the collection  of royalties. Moreover, 
and in addition, the communication an d publication of that decision, in  principle set the time limit for 
certain persons to bring appeals before this Board of Appeal, as set out in Article 69 of the Basic 
Regulation. It therefore follows that the deci sion on 29 September 2021, and the subsequent 
communication and publication of that decision and the change to the Regi ster itself, directly affected the 
legal situation of the Appellant.  
 84. Given the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that  the decision to update the Register on 29 September 
2021, and the subsequent communication and publication of that decision and the change to the Register 
itself, is of direct concern to the Appellant.    
 85. As concerns individual concern, it is settled case la w that persons other than those to whom a decision is 
addressed may only claim to be individually concerned if that decision affects them by reason of certain 
attributes which are peculiar to them or by reason of  circumstances in which they are differentiated from 
all other persons and by virtue of these factors distingu ishes them individually just  as in the case of the 
person addressed. See Judgment of the Eu ropean Court of Justice, 14 July 2022, 
Italian Republic, Comune 
di Milano v Council of the European Union, European Parliament , Joined Cases C-106/19 and C-232/19, 
ECLI:EU:C:2022:568, paragraph 68 and the caselaw cited therein).  
 86. In this case there are certain attributes which ar e peculiar to the Appellant and/or the circumstances, 
which mean the Appellant is differentiated from all ot her persons. Specifically, it was the Appellant whose 
name and address were deleted from the Register as  regards the Queen PVR and whose particulars, in 
this regard, were replaced by those of SGI. Moreover, it is by virtue of these factors that distinguishes 
the Appellant individually in this case. Specifically, by virtue of the decision of 29 September 2021 to 
update the Register, the Appellant alone, lost its list ing as the holder of the right for the Queen PVR on 
the Register and, as a result, its ability to asse rt legitimate ownership over the Queen PVR itself. 
 
87.   Given the above, the Board of Appeal concludes th at the decision to update the particulars of the Queen 
PVR on the Register on 29 September 2021 is of individual concern to the Appellant. 
 
88. The Board of Appeal therefore finds that the Appellan t as an addressee, and as a legal person with direct 
and individual concern, is entitled to bring an appeal pursuant to Ar ticle 68 of the Basic Regulation.  
 89. As regards the time limit for the lodgement of an appeal, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has 
not contested that the Appellant has lodged its Notice of  Appeal before the time limit set out in Article 69 
of the Basic Regulation. Article 69 of the Basic Regulation provides that a Notice of Appeal must be lodged 
at the Office “…within two months of the service of the decision where addressed to the appealing 
person…”. The service of the decision in this case must be regarded as the first formal communication by the CPVO to the Appellant informing the Appellant of th e deletion and entry of information on the Register 
concerning the Queen PVR and relating  to the rightsholder information – namely the letter sent by the 
CPVO to the Appellant of 14 December 2021. As stat ed above at paragraph [28], the Appellant lodged 
the Notice of Appeal on 11 February 2022. The Board of Appeal therefore concludes that the Appellant 
lodged the Notice of Appeal before the relevant deadline.   
 90. The second part of Article 69 of the Basic Regulation provides that “[in the absence of the service of the 
decision addressed to the person appealing], [the Notice of appeal shall be filed in writing at the Office] 
within two months of the publication of the decision. ” To the extent that this deadline could be regarded 
as of relevance, the Board of Appeal notes that th e date of the publication of Gazette concerning the 
change in the listed rightsholder for the Queen PV R was 15 December 2021. As stated above at paragraph 
[28], the Appellant lodged the Notice of Appeal on  11 February 2022. The Board of Appeal therefore 
concludes that the Appellant lodged the Notice  of Appeal before the relevant deadline. 
 
91. Having regard to the above, the Board of Appeal  concludes that the present appeal case meets the 
relevant conditions concerning admissibility, and that the appeal is therefore admissible.

V.  SUBSTANCE 
 94.  In support of its appeal, the Appellant relies on a number of pleas and arguments, including the following: 
(1) The CPVO did not properly consider the informat ion and evidence before registering the transfer 
of the Queen PVR on the Register.  
(2) The CPVO did not give the Appellant sufficien t warning of the registration of the transfer. 
(3) The CPVO failed to stop, suspend or dela y the decision to register the transfer.  
(4)  The CPVO failed to sufficiently state the reas ons for the decision to register the transfer.  
 95. As a preliminary issue, and before assessing the me rits of the pleas and arguments put forward by the 
Parties, the Board of Appeal recalls, that the Board of Appeal is strictly limited and restricted in the scope 
of its review. More specifically, the Board of Appeal is limited and restricted to  assessing certain action 
and inaction taken by the CPVO in specific fields. Th e Board of Appeal has no legal or other powers to 
review issues outside of its competencies. Issues such  as alleged theft, alleged deceitful or fraudulent 
activities, and/or other related alleged non bona fide an d criminal activities on th e part of natural or legal 
persons, are generally outside the scope of the CPVO  Board of Appeal competencies, and therefore not 
issues the CPVO Board of Appeal can or will opine. As such, the Board of Appeal  shall, in these appeal 
proceedings, strictly adhere to the scope and remit of  its powers, and assess only  those issues within its 
mandate to review which, in this appeal case, prim arily concern action and inaction taken by the CPVO 
as regards the registration of the transfer of th e Queen PVR from the Appellant company to SGI. The 
CPVO Board of Appeal remains cognizant of the fact that whilst the appeal proceedings before the CPVO 
Board of Appeal have been ongoing in this case, there are legal proceed ings also ongoing before national 
Courts in one of the EU Member States. For the avoi dance of doubt, the assessments and findings of the 
CPVO Board of Appeal in this appeal case have not been impacted, and do not seek to impact or affect, 
in any way whatsoever, the assessments and findings by other EU or national Courts, or other bodies. 
 
First plea alleging the CPVO did not properly consider the relevant information and evidence  
 96. By this plea the Appellant essentially alleges that: (1) The CPVO failed to comp ly with the duty of care, 
and carry out a careful and diligent examination of al l the facts and circumstances, before adopting the 
decision to change the particulars on the Register co ncerning the PVR and/or the decision to transfer the 
register; and (2) The CPVO failed to take into consid eration all the information that the CPVO was required 
to consider before adopting the decision to change  the particulars on the Register concerning the PVR 
and/or the decision to register the transfer. 
 97. The CPVO, together with the Interv ener, rejects this plea and the related lines of argument as unfounded. 
According to the CPVO, the onus is on titleholders  to keep the CPVO up-to-date on the individuals 
nominated to act on their behalf. Where a titleholder is  a legal entity which has appointed a representative, 
and where that individual no longer has the legal author ity to represent or act on behalf of the legal entity 

     14 
 
in question, it is, accordin g to the CPVO, incumbent on the legal entity in questi on to immediately inform 
the CPVO of that fact. According to  the CPVO, there is a ‘presumption of validity’ meaning that the CPVO 
should be able to presume that all persons with whom  it interacts with – act in good faith.  The CPVO

Findings of the Board of Appeal  
 98. As a preliminary issue, and before assessing the me rits of the plea and arguments put forward by the 
Parties, the Board of Appeal recalls th e general legal framework and guidance.  
 
99. Firstly, the Board of Appeal recalls the general le gal framework and guidance concerning the registration 
of a transfer of a PVR which includes, bu t is not limited, to the following.  
 100. Article 23 of the Basic Regulation states:   
Article 23 
Transfer 
1. A Community plant variety right may be the object of  a transfer to one or more  successors in title.  
2. Transfer of a Community plant variety right by assignment can be made only to successors who comply 
with the conditions laid down in Arti cle 12 and 82. It shall be made in wr iting and shall require the signature 
of the parties to the contract, except  when it is a result of a judgem ent or of any other acts terminating 
court proceedings. Otherwise it shall be void.  
3. Save as otherwise provided in Article 100, a transfer shall have no bearing on the rights acquired by 
third parties before the date of transfer. 
4. A transfer shall not take effect for the Office  and may not be cited vis-à-vis third parties unless 
documentary evidence thereof as pr ovided for in the implementing rule s is provided and until it has been 
entered in the Register of Community  Plant Variety Rights. A transfer t hat has not yet been entered in the 
Register may, however, be cited vis-à-vis third parties who have acquired rights after the date of transfer 
but who knew of the transfe r at the date on which they acquired those rights.” 
 
101. Article 79 of the IRR states:  
Article 79 
Entry of transfer of a Community plant variety right 
1. Any transfer of Community plant variety rights sha ll be entered in the Register of Community Plant 
Variety Rights on production of document ary evidence of the transfer, or of official documents confirming 
the transfer, or of such extracts fr om those documents as suffice to es tablish the transfer.  The Office shall 
retain a copy of those pieces of documentary evidence in its files.  
The President of the Office shall determine the form in and the conditions under wh ich those pieces of 
documentary evidence are to be retain ed in the files of the Office.  
2. The entry of a transfer may be refused only in the event of failure to comply with the conditions laid 
down in paragraph 1 and in Article 23 of the basic Regulation.  
3. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall apply to any transfer of an entitlement to a Community plant variety right for 
which an application has been entered in the Register of Applications for Community Plant Variety Rights. 
The reference to the Register of Community Plant Variety Rights shall be understood as a reference to 
the Register of Applications fo r Community Plant Variety Rights. 
 
102. CPVO Guidance, as set out on the CPVO website und er “FAQs” or Frequently Asked Questions, states:
1   
 How to register a transfer of CPVR ownership? 
A deed of assignment whereby the present holder (a ssignor) transfers the concerned CPVRs or/and 
application(s) to the new holder (assignee) must be provided. It must be duly dated and signed by both 
holders or parties. A template assignment is available on our website at:  
http://cpvo.europa.eu/en/appl ications-and-examinations /filing-application/filing- paper. Please note that 
there is a fee of € 100 for the transfer recordal and a publication in the Offici al Gazette of the Office. 
 
103. The CPVO document entitl ed “Assignments - Validity”2 states, amongst other things, that: “ Please note 
that: 1. There is no official assignm ent form. 2. In view of its laisse z-faire approach, the Office respects 
the contractual will of the parties involved. 3. The Office does not encourage anyone to  renounce to their 
rights ” and that “ The deed of assignment required consists of any  valid act of disposal of a right by its 
holder, as, for instance, a contract, a declaration of as signment, a letter, a will or any other document that 
serves the purpose of transfer of a right  from its rightful owner to another party. ” That document also sets

legal person (e.g. companies) do not forget to mention the name of the natural person who has

validity of the signature(s) pr esent on the document filed.  
2. The breeder's reference or the de nomination of the plant variety.  
3. The botanical taxon of the plant variety.  
4. The territory concerned, namely where the title is valid (European Union).  
5. A clearly worded transfer of the entitlement to apply for Plant  Breeders’ rights or of the 
application for registration or of the registered Plant Breeders’ ri ght, if it was already granted. 
Please, bear in mind that a mere licence is not a valid assignment for our purposes, as it provides 
a permission to benefit from a certain right  without any transfer of ownership.  
6. The deed of assignment shall be dated.  
7. If the breeder is an employee, the entitlement to the Community  plant variety right shall be 
determined in accordance with the national law applic able to the employment relationship in the 
context of which the variety was bred, or discovered and developed, as indicated under Article 
11(4) of Council Regulation 2100/94. To that effe ct, the relevant nationa l law applicable should 
be precisely cited.  
8. A digital copy of the relevant document is suffi cient. However, the original or a certified copy 
of the deed will be required in case of doubt.  
9. The assignment must also c ontain the signatures of both parties if it  is made during the 
application procedure or after a title has been granted (Articles 23 and 26 Council Regulation 
2100/94). If the assignment is carried out before an app lication for a Plant Variety right is filed to 
the CPVO, the obligation of signat ure should be assessed pursuant to the national law applicable 
to the assignment. It is, in any case, advisable that both parties sign the assignment.  
 
104. Although not strictly forming part of the legal fram ework and guidance relating to PVRs per se, the Board 
of Appeal does take note of the EUIPO Trademark and Design guidelines, in cluding the current EUIPO 
guidelines,3 and EUIPO guidelines in place at the time the CPVO registered the transfer of the Queen PVR 
in this case. As regards the latter, the Board of Appeal notes that certain parts of the EUIPO Trade Mark 
Guidelines4 clearly reflected the importance of the duty of diligence (see paragraph [111] below), and set 
out ways to best ensure it is discharged in practice, stating, for example: “ A transfer may be registered 
only when it is proven by document s duly establishing the transfer […] ” and that “ If the Office has reason 
to doubt the accuracy or veracity of th e document, it may require additional proof ”.  
 105. Secondly, the Board of Appeal recalls the gene ral legal framework and guidance concerning the 
appointment of representatives which includes, but is not limited, to the following.   
 106. Article 82 of the Basic Regulation states: “ Persons who are not domiciled or do not have a seat or an 
establishment within the territory of  the Community may participate as  party to proceedings before the 
Office only if they have designated a procedural representative who is domiciled or has his seat or an 
establishment within the te rritory of the Community. ” Articles 73-74, and 66a of the IRR, amongst others, 
regulate the affairs of procedural representatives incl uding their credentials, de signation and notifications 
to procedural representatives.  
 107. Article 2(4) of the IRR states: “ 4. Where a party to proceedings is a legal person, it shall also be 
designated by the name and address of the natural pers on legally representing the party to proceedings 
by virtue of the relevant national legislation. The provisions of  paragraph 2 shall apply mutatis mutandis 
to such natural person. The Office may permit derogations  from the provisions of the first sentence of the 
first subparagraph. ”

Article 73  
Designation of a procedural representative

111. In addition to the general legal framework and guid ance, some of which is set out above, the Board of 
Appeal recalls the relevant jurisprudence. More specific ally, that it is settled case-law that, when the CPVO 
or other relevant EU agency assess whether to register the transfer of a PVR or similar IP right, the CPVO 
is required to examine carefully and impartially all th e relevant factual and legal aspects of the individual 
case (see, General Court Judgment of 22 September 2021, Marina Yachting Brand Management Co. Ltd 
v EUIPO , Case T-169/20, ECLI:EU:T:2021:609, hereinafter “M arina Yachting”, paragraph 58 and the case-
law cited therein). More specifically, it is settled case -law that the CPVO must, in these circumstances, act 
in accordance with the duty of di ligence (see, by analogy, Marina Yachting, paragraphs 69, 74, 58 and 
the paragraphs referred to therein). Pursuant to that  duty, it is incumbent up on the CPVO to amongst 
other things “[…] diligently take into account facts that ar e capable of having legal implications for the 
entries it makes in that register ” (see Case T-169/20, paragraph 58).
