112. In light of the jurisprudence, the Board of Appeal  finds it necessary to assess whether the CPVO fully 
discharged its duty of diligence before registering the transfer of the Queen PVR, and in particular deleting 
and entering the relevant particulars on the Register  regarding the Queen PVR. In light of the relevant 
legal framework and guidance, the Board of Appeal no tes the following initial points in this regard. 
 113. Owners of PVRs have freedom as regards their transf er and passing on. They, in their discretion, are able 
to determine if and how a PVR is tr ansferred in accordance with releva nt law. As such, the legal transfer 
of a PVR is an issue primarily governed, in the first in stance, by the legal rules and requirements applicable 
under relevant national Member State and/or other law.  Where disputes arise, it is generally an issue for 
national justice systems, in the first in stance, to resolve. The CPVO has not, 
per se , been afforded legal 
powers or competencies to regulate, dictate and/or  determine how a PVR right is legally transferred. 
Moreover, as a general principle, the CPVO is not, per se , required to rule on contractual or legal questions 
arising under national law in determ ining transfer of a PVR right (see, to that effect and by analogy, 
General Court Judgment of 9 September 2011, David Chalk v Office for Harmonisation in the Internal 
Market (CRAIC) , Case T-83/09, EU:T:2011:450, paragraph 27) . As recognised in, for example, EUIPO 
guidelines concerning trademarks, where concerns may arise as to the legal ownership of a PVR – it would 
generally be for the relevant national justice system(s ), in the first instance, to address the legality and 
lawfulness of a transfer and legal ownership of the PVR itself (see “Trade Mark Guidelines, Part E Register 
Operations, Section 3 EUTMS and RCDS as objects of property, Chapter 1 Transfer, 4 requirements for 
an application for registration of a transfer, Paragraph 3.5 ‘Proof of transfer’ which states “[…] If doubts 
arise, the national courts deal with the legality of the transfer itself ”).   
 
114. At the same time, it cannot be overlooked, that th e CPVO has been afforded exclusive legal powers and 
competencies to implement and manage the EU PVR sy stem. A critical component of the EU PVR system 
is the Register. The Register, and the information it co ntains, is essential in ensuring the EU PVR system 
functions as legally intended. Moreover, it is essentia l in maintaining the credibili ty and confidence in the 
PVR system itself.  The CPVO alone has been entrus ted with ensuring the Register, and the information 
contained within it, is correct and up to date. Of the information contained in the Register, data concerning 
the ownership of a PVR is particularly important. Re cording the correct transfer of ownership of a PVR 
from one natural or legal person to another, and keepin g the Register up to date in this regard, is a core 
task of the CPVO of essential impo rtance to the EU PVR system. As the custodian of the Register, it is 
therefore incumbent upon the CPVO to  ensure it takes all proportionat e steps to ensure the information 
contained in the Register regarding a transf er, is correct and can be relied upon.  
 115. It follows from the above, that before registering a transfer of a PVR in the Register, and in accordance 
with its duty of diligence, it is in cumbent on the CPVO to ensure, amon gst other things: (1) that the CPVO 
has the necessary information it needs to change the relevant details in the Register (e.g. the names of 
the transferor and transferee, date, name of PVR, name  and contact details of th e relevant individuals or 
persons acting as representatives to companies, etc); and (2) it has the necessary and sufficient 
documentary evidence proving the transfer in law an d fact (e.g. signed deeds / agreements / contracts 
to transfer the PVR etc) - and supporting document ation (e.g. signed deeds / agreements / contracts 
appointing representatives to represent a company or legal entity, and extracts from (business) registers 
or official documents signed by relevant independen t witnesses proving an indi vidual has the right to 
represent a company or legal entity). Moreover, when assessing the information and documentary 
evidence available, it is settled case-law that the CP VO must take into account all the relevant factual and 
legal aspects of the individual case (see, Marina Ya chting, paragraph 58 and th e case-law cited therein). 
As such, the CPVO must consider, amongst other thin gs, whether there are any grounds for a potential 
concern. Where such grounds exist, it is incumbent on  the CPVO to take further or additional steps to 
resolve and clarify those issues before changes to the Register are made. This may include, as stated in 
the EUIPO Guidelines referred to ab ove at paragraph [104], obtaining “
additional proof ” of the transfer 
and/or representation of a legal entity.  
 116. In light of the above, the Board of Appeal finds it  necessary to consider whether the CPVO discharged its 
duty of diligence before changing the details in th e Register concerning the Queen PVR, taking into 
account the pertinent facts and the pleas and arguments brought forward by the Parties. 
 117. As stated above at paragraph [4], on 23 Septembe r 2021, the CPVO advised Mr Renán that, in order for 
the CPVO to change the Register to reflect the appa rent transfer of the Queen PVR – the CPVO “Option 
B” form concerning the “
Assignment of (an) application(s) or (a) registered Community Plant Variety 
Right(s) ”7 should be used. As stated above at paragraphs [7-9], on 29 September 2021, Mr Renán then 
 
7 https://cpvo.europa.eu/sites/default/file s/documents/assignment_template_en_.pdf   

     18 
 
provided an Option B form containing certain inform ation (“Option B Form”). As also stated above at 
paragraph [23], on 14 December 2021 the CPVO confir med that the registration of the transfer of 
ownership “ was registered on the basis of the documen t provided on 29 September 2021 (model ES — 
option B) ”.   
 
118. In these appeal proceedings, the CPVO has asserted that because Mr Renán provided the CPVO the Option 
B Form containing certain information, Mr Renán was not required to provide the CPVO with “ any [other] 
additional documentary evidence ”. The CPVO has stated that “…a duly completed and signed transfer 
form from the [CPVO] constitutes a perfectly valid  instrument to prove the assignment for which 
registration is sought ”. The CPVO takes the view that this appr oach is consistent with Article 23 of the 
Basic Regulation and Article 79 of the IRR which, according to the CPVO, require a written document 
bearing a digital or other signature. Should the CP VO require other information or documentation this 
would, according to the CPVO, “ be tantamount to creating de facto r equirements that are exogenous to 
the applicable legislation ”. The CPVO has stated that requirin g stakeholders to provide documentary 
evidence that, for example, a specific individual has been validly appointed as a representative of a legal 
entity, is not in accordance with the EU legislature’ s intent and that requiring this type of documentary 
evidence “…would even be contra legem, to the extent that  the EU legislator wanted to design a system 
with a relatively “low” evidentiary threshold, simple  and general, which in any case would not be onerous ".  
 
119. The Board of Appeal finds that these argu ments cannot succeed and should be rejected. 
 120. First, and as stated above at paragraph [111], the CP VO must act in accordance with the duty of diligence 
in proceedings concerning the transfer of a PVR, and be fore registering a transfer.  If a person were able 
to legally dictate and automatically require the CPVO to register a transfer of a PVR in the Register merely 
by, for example, providing a CPVO template form cont aining certain information with a digital or other 
signature, it would impede on the CPVO’s ability to co mply with the CPVO’s duty of diligence, and call into 
question the ability for the CPVO to ensure the info rmation in the Register is  accurate, up-to-date, and 
complete - and able to be relied upon. 
 121. Second, pursuant to the duty of  diligence, it is incumbent on th e CPVO to take all relevant and 
proportionate steps to ensure it fully  discharges that duty before changing the details of ownership of a 
PVR in the Register. Although the CP VO may in its discretion determine the precise steps to be taken to 
discharge the duty of diligence – the Board of Appeal  notes that, in principle, the CPVO should ensure, 
amongst other things, that the documentary evidence provided is up-to-date and complete, and does not 
call into question or raise clear concerns or doubts regarding, for example: (1) the transfer of a PVR, 
and/or (2) the ability for an individual to represent a legal entity during the CPVO  proceedings in question. 
As such, in this case, it was genera lly incumbent on the CPVO to ensu re that the documentary evidence 
was up-to-date and complete, and did not raise clear concerns or doubts concerning: (1) the apparent/purported transfer of the Queen PVR from th e Appellant to SGI, and (2) the ability of Mr Renán 
to represent the Appellant and the SGI in CPVO proc eedings concerning the transfer of the Queen PVR 
from the Appellant.   
 122. Third, Article 79 of the IRR requires that the tr ansfer of PVRs shall be entered in the Register “ on the 
production of ” certain documentary evidence. It is theref ore for the CPVO to determine what precise 
documentary evidence the relevant persons must prod uce and provide the CPVO, in light of all the facts 
and legal aspects of an individual case, so as to en able the CPVO to update and change the Register. The 
CPVO must exercise its discretionary powers in accordance with EU law, including the duty of diligence and the principle of proportionality. Where there is a heightened concern or risk due, for example, to a 
potential conflict of interest – it may be necessary  for the CPVO to take additional steps (including 
obtaining additional documentation or assurances from  the relevant parties) to discharge its duty of 
diligence before registering a transfer.  
 123. Fourth, the primary legal intent of the CPVO templa te Option B form must be clearly understood in its 
legal context. The primary legal intent of the CPVO te mplate Option B form is not to ensure that parties 
are able to transfer a PVR in all national jurisdic tions throughout the European Union, the European 
Economic Area and/or beyond. Whether, in a specific situation – such as the one in this case – a particular 
document based on the Option B template and contai ning certain information – is legally capable of 
transferring a PVR under relevant national law and ha s legally done so, is, in the first instance, for the 
national justice systems to determine. As such, whethe r the Option B Form as provided by Mr Renán is 
capable or transferring, and/or did transfer – on its own and/or in light of other documentation and facts 
– the Queen PVR is a question primarily for the Spanish national justice system to determine. Nonetheless, 
the primary intention of the CPVO Option B form itse lf, and the primary objective of stakeholders filling 
in this template form and providing it to the CPVO – is to facilitate the CPVO in deciding whether, in 

     19 
 
accordance with its duty of diligence, the CPVO is ab le to change the relevant details on the Register. As 
stated above at paragraph [113], the CPVO has not, per se , been afforded legal powers or competencies 
to regulate or dictate how a PVR right is legally tran sferred under national Member State or other law. As 
such, a completed Option B form must be generally rega rded as a document intended primarily to facilitate 
the CPVO in understanding and examining the relevant facts and evidence in acco rdance with the duty of 
diligence, and to help the CPVO ascertain whether it is  legally possible for the CPVO to register the transfer 
in the Register – not as a document primarily intended  to effectuate the legal transfer of a PVR under, 
for example, Spanish national law.  
 124. Lastly, as stated above, the intention of the legislat ure was to establish a Register, forming part of an EU 
PVR system, which stakeholders could trust and rely upon. The CPVO’s role in ensuring the information 
contained within the Register is accurate, up-to-date and reliable is therefore crit ical. Taking proportionate 
steps and ensuring stakeholders provide relevant do cumentary evidence to ensure the system is not 
subject to abuse cannot be regarded as inconsistent with the intention of the legislature or the CPVO’s 
duty of diligence. In this regard, th e Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO itself has more recently required 
the Appellant to provide certain documentary evidence  in order to effectuate the registration of the 
transfer of the PVR back to the Appellant. This includes, for example, documentation concerning 
“appointment of members of the b oard of directors of the company Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A., which 
you have provided us with the signatures of the interested parties. - An extract from the Mercantile Registry 
stating the change in the co mpany's administrative body ” - see paragraph [15] above. The Board of Appeal 
note that it could be regarded as inconsistent to, on  the one hand, assert that the CPVO is not required 
to obtain this information and documentary evidence (and  assert that to do so would be inconsistent with 
the legislature’s intention) and, on the other ha nd, require that same or similar information and 
documentary evidence in order to effectuate the regi stration of the transfer of the PVR back to the 
Appellant company.   
 125. It follows from the above that merely because Mr Re nán had provided an Option B Form containing certain 
information bearing a digital stamp – this did not mean  the CPVO was able, or required, to automatically 
update the details of the Register concerning the ow nership of the Queen PVR. Instead, it was incumbent 
on the CPVO to understand, amongst other things, wh ether: (1) the information in this document was 
correct, reliable, and complete – or whether, for exampl e, it gave rise to a potential concern; and/or (2) 
whether the information in this document (including th e information on the apparent transfer of the PVR) 
was sufficiently substantiated and support ed by documentary evidence.      
 126. Given the points set out above, the Board of Appeal initially concludes that the duty of diligence applied 
in this case. Receipt of the Option  B Form by the CPVO did not mean that the CPVO was discharged from 
its duty of diligence or that this requirement had been  satisfied.  Instead, it was incumbent on the CPVO, 
in accordance with its duty of diligence, to examin e carefully and impartially al l the relevant factual and 
legal aspects of the individual case and, amongst other things, to: (1) check the information it had 
received in the Option B Form and otherwise, (2) as certain whether it had all the information necessary 
to effectuate the registration of the transfer, and (3) then ensure it  had all the necessary and sufficient 
documentary evidence.  
 127. In addition to the pleas and arguments summarised above, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has 
also put forward arguments asserting that, at the ti me the CPVO registered the transfer of the Queen 
PVR in the Register, the CPVO was able to rely on a ‘presumption of validity’ pursuant to which the CPVO 
could presume there had been a valid transfer of th e Queen PVR. According to the CPVO, the name and 
contact details of the individual apparently representing  the transferor and transferee legal entities in this 
case had already been inserted into the CPVO IT syst ems as a representative of the transferor company. 
The individual was known to the CPVO and had been ac ting on behalf of the Appellant in the past as 
regards the PVR in question vis-à- vis the payment of annual fees et c. And the CPVO had documentation 
to support a presumption of validity.  
 128. The Board of Appeal finds that these argu ments cannot succeed and should be rejected. 
 129. First, and as stated above, it is settled case-law that before registering a transfer the CPVO is under a 
duty of diligence. As such, in  this case, the CPVO was required to discharge that duty before registering 
the transfer. Any potential presumption of validity cannot be regarded as negating the duty of diligence, or as rendering the duty of diligence as null and void.   
 130. Second, the CPVO has not brought forward sufficient proof that a presum ption of validity could exist in 
this case. As clarified at the oral hearing, Mr Renán is not, and was not at the time he worked for and 
represented the Appellant company, a lawyer, an a ccountant or other professional – and therefore was 

     20 
 
not, per se, required to comply with a set of professi onal or ethical rules such as a duty to act in good 
faith. Also, and as set out in more detail at paragrap hs [131-134] below, in this case there was reason to 
believe Mr Renán may have a personal vested interest  in registering the transfer which might override 
ethical, moral, professional or other considerations. To  the extent that the CPVO takes the view that – as 
Mr Renán was a director or officer of the Appellant company – the CPVO could presume that Mr Renán was bound by a fiduciary duty to act in the best in terests of the Appellant company, and therefore that 
Mr Renán acted in a bona fide manner; the Board of Appeal notes that this pre-supposes that Mr Renán 
was a director or officer of the company at the ti me Mr Renán submitted the request to register the 
transfer. It was therefore all the more important for the CPVO to take proportionate steps to ascertain, 
with sufficient and up-to-date suppo rting documentary evidence, that: (1) Mr Renán was a natural person 
currently serving as an  director, officer or other relevant person for the Appellant company and (2) Mr 
Renán provided up-to-date documentary proof that he  served the Appellant as a current director and 
officer, and had the express legal right and authorisat ion from the Appellant company to represent it in 
important proceedings before the CP VO involving the transfer of the Queen PVR to a third party. The 
Board of Appeal addresses this issue at  paragraphs [135- 149] below.     
 131. Lastly, insofar as any presumption of validity could have initially existed – that presumption did not exist 
after Mr Renán had provided the Option B Form on  29 September 2021. That was because the Option B 
Form provided by Mr Renán made clear that Mr Renán was seeking to act alone and represent two 
separate legal entities (companies) at the same time, where the two compan ies in question had, 
prima 
facie, conflicting interests.  
 132. As is clear, and as the CPVO has correctly pointed ou t in its written submissions during this appeal case, 
a transfer of a PVR, and the rights associated with a PVR, is generally to the detriment of the transferor 
and to the benefit of the transferee. This means that  the interests of the transferor and transferee are 
therefore not aligned – but conflict. Natural persons seeking to act for two separate legal persons, where 
one is the transferor and the other the transferee, are therefore themselves – most likely to be conflicted 
and unable to act unless additional explanation and safeguards are put in  place. It may, for example, be 
possible to address and resolve potent ial conflicts if there is a transfer  between affiliates within the same 
corporate family (e.g. transfers as between parent /holding companies and their subsidiaries, or as 
between twin/sister legal entities) and safeguards ar e put in place (e.g. additional assurances, from 
additional natural persons, along with  evidential proof from the entities involved). However, in the absence 
of any information, assurances, or documentary evidence  that there is no conflict of interest and/or no 
relevant concern – the Board of Appeal finds that it is  not possible to invoke or rely upon a ‘presumption 
of validity’ or ‘presumption of good faith’.  
 133. In this case, the information provided in the Op tion B Form on 29 September 2021 clearly indicated an 
actual or potential conflict of interest existed. Desp ite this clear potential concern, the CPVO failed to 
obtain additional information, assu rances or documentary evidence to address the issue. Where there is 
a clear potential conflict of interest, and where no ac tion is taken to address or resolve that obvious 
concern – it is self-evident that the CPVO cannot seek to invoke or rely on a purported presumption of validity to leave the issue unresolv ed. Instead, in such situations, th e CPVO must comply all the most 
strictly with its duty of diligence and take further steps to address and resolve the apparent conflict of 
interest in question. This is consis tent with the duty of diligence to examine carefully and impartially all 
the relevant factual and legal aspects of the individual case.         
 
134. Given the above points, the Board of Appeal initially concludes that it was incumb ent on the CPVO to fully 
discharge its duty of diligence in this case by taking  into account all the relevant factual and legal aspects 
of the individual case (see, Mari na Yachting, paragraph 58 and the case-law cited therein). The CPVO 
could not rely upon a presumption of validity as, am ongst other things, there we re clear and unresolved 
concerns - in particular a concern regarding a conflict  of interest. Moreover, the Board of Appeal initially 
concludes that by not addressing issues of clear conc ern – such as the apparent conflict of interest, and 
by registering the transfer before these clear concerns  were resolved – the CPVO did not act in accordance 
with is duty of diligence. By, for example, not taking  steps to address or resolve the issue of concern by 
obtaining “ additional proof ” or assurances that there was no conflic t of interest, or that safeguards had 
been put in place to address the apparent conflict – the Board of Appeal initially concludes that the CPVO 
acted in breach of this duty. 
 135. In addition to the pleas and arguments summarised above, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has 
also brought forward arguments asserting that, in th is case, the CPVO had rece ived documentation which 
was sufficient to permit the regi stration of the transfer.   
 

     21 
 
136. Before assessing these arguments in detail, the Boar d of Appeal recalls the foll owing points. Firstly, and 
has stated above (see paragraph [111]), the CPVO mu st adhere to, and fully discharge, its duty of 
diligence in proceedings concerning the registration of a transfer.  Secondly, and as also stated above 
(see paragraph [115]), pursuant to the duty of diligence it is incumbent on the CPVO to ensure it has the 
necessary and sufficient documentary evidence to register a transfer. As regards the precise nature and 
scope of the documentary evidence re quired to register a transfer, th is will depend on the factual and 
other circumstances surrounding a particular case.  Howe ver, and in any event, the Board of Appeal notes 
that the CPVO must take proportionate steps to en sure the documentary evidence is, as stated at 
paragraph [121] above, up-to-date and complete, and do es not call into question or raise clear concerns 
or doubts regarding, for example: (1) the transfer of  a PVR, and/or (2) the ability for an individual to 
represent a legal entity during th e CPVO proceedings in question. 
 137. As regards the documentation concerning representati on of the legal entities, the Board of Appeal takes 
the view that it is particularly important for the CP VO to ensure that the documentary evidence does not 
raise clear concerns and doubts regarding the natural person representing the transferor entity. That is 
because, following the registration of the transfer, the transferor en tity will likely lose its ability to 
legitimately claim the benefits associated with owne rship of the PVR. As such, and taking these general 
points into consideration, the Board of Appeal notes the following.   
 138. Firstly, in this case, Mr Renán was appointed as a representative of the Appellant company, for certain 
CPVO procedures, pursuant to a Procedural Represen tative Form signed and completed by Mr Renán on 
12 April 2006. Mr Renán submitted the request for th e CPVO to change the Register on 29 September 
2021. The relevant documentation in place – was th erefore more than 15 years old. Taking into 
consideration the importance of having up-to-date do cumentation in place concerning the representation 
of the transferor company, and taki ng into consideration the fact that the CPVO has not brought forward 
sufficient reasons to justify the apparent absence of such up-to-date documentati on in this case – the 
Board of Appeal concludes that the documentation in place in this rega rd was not sufficiently up-to-date. 
 
139. Secondly, the Procedural Representative Form of 12  April 2006 completed and signed by Mr Renán states, 
amongst other things:  
 
 


     22 
 
 
 
140. The Board of Appeal notes, in particular, that th e form states that Mr Renán was the representative “ con 
relación a; mi solicitud de una protección Comuni taria de las obtenciones vegetales; titularidad de una 
protección Comunitaria de las obtenciones vegetales ”. It therefore follows that this form only appointed 
Mr Renán to represent the Appellant before the CP VO insofar as the proceedings related to: (1) the 
Appellant’s application for the Queen PVR; and (2) th e Appellant’s ownership of the Queen PVR. That is 
to say that the form only seemingly intended to appo int Mr Renán as the representative of the Appellant 
company in proceedings before the CPVO insofar as those proceedings served to preserve and protect 
the Appellant’s ownership of the PVR and not for other proceedings - such as the registered transfer from 
the Appellant to a third party.  
 141. In this regard the Board of Appeal notes that  on 11 January 2016 the CPVO adopted a new template 
document concerning the representation of  legal persons by natural individuals.
8 The new template 
document was revised to include with in it, amongst other things, new an d additional legal wording which 
specifically addressed situations concerning the representation of legal persons in CPVO proceedings 
regarding the loss of title/ownership of a PVR – incl uding, therefore, CPVO proceedings regarding the 
registration of a transfer of a PVR. In particular , the current and up-to-date version of the template 
Procedural Representative Form in Spanish states: 
 
 
  142. The Board of Appeal notes, in particular, that th e new template form therefore currently states that a 
representative is appointed in respect of: “ Todos los procedimientos ( incluyendo cesiones de titulos ) 
con relación a mi(s)/nuestro(s) solicitud(es) y derecho(s). Todos los procedimientos ( incluyendo 
cesiones de titulos ) con relación a mi(s)/nuestro(s) solicitud(e s) y derecho(s) a las siguientes especies 
 
8 https://cpvo.europa.eu/en/news-an d-events/news/new-designation- procedural-representative-form   


     23 
 
[…]” (emphasis added). The Board of Appeal notes, ther efore, that the template form was revised by the 
CPVO to include legal wording which the CPVO thought sufficiently impo rtant to necessitate revision of, 
and inclusion in, the new template form. The Board of Appeal also notes that, in this case, the CPVO did 
not require the Appellant and/or Mr Renán to prov ide a new document, based on the new template.  
Moreover, the CPVO did not require the Appellant to provide a new document based on the new template, 
even though the new legal wording in cluded in the new template docume nt regarding cessation of title, 
was directly relevant to the CPVO procedure in question (i.e. registration of a transfer).   
 143. As such, and in short, on the basi s of the documentation made available to the Board of Appeal, it appears, 
in this case, that the documentation in place regard ing representation of the Appellant consisted of a 
document based on an old and outdated template  and which omitted important legal wording. The 
omission of that legal wording raises a clear concern and doubt as to whether Mr Renán has ever been appointed to represent the Appellant company in proceed ings concerning the registration of a transfer of 
the Queen PVR to third parties. From the documentat ion provided to the Board of Appeal, it is not 
apparent that the Appellant company has ever appoin ted Mr Renán as a representative before the CPVO 
as regards procedures involving the transfer or assi gnment of the Queen PVR to  third parties. The Board 
of Appeal therefore concludes that the documentation in place in this regard was not sufficiently robust 
or complete and could not be relied upon to register the transfer.  
 144. Lastly, the Board of Appeal note that the su fficiency and completeness of documentary evidence 
concerning representation – and enabling the registration  of a transfer – should be considered in light of 
all the facts and circumstances. In this case, the Bo ard of Appeal note, amongst other things, that: (1) 
the natural person purporting to represent the transf eror legal entity (i.e. Mr Renán) – had apparently, 
on the basis of the available docume ntation – appointed himself to act on behalf of the transferor legal 
entity (i.e. he/she had not been appointed to act fo r the company by different people working for or on 
behalf of the transferor legal entity ), (2) the natural person purporting  to represent the transferor legal 
entity (i.e. Mr Renán) was also seeking to act on behalf of the transferee company and therefore in a 
situation where a conflict of interest apparently existe d where personal interests could override ethical or 
professional obligations, and (3) no other natural person  working for, or on behalf of, the transferor legal 
entity had, on the basis of the ava ilable documentation, apparently cons ented or approved of the transfer. 
In these circumstances, the Board of Appeal notes th at it was all-the-more im portant for the CPVO to 
ensure the documentary evidence of valid and current representation of  transferor legal entity by Mr 
Renán - was sufficiently up-to-date and complete. As th e CPVO has not brought forward relevant evidence 
to prove that the documentary evidence was sufficient to permit registration of the transfer in these 
circumstances, the Board of Appeal concludes that there was an absence of such documentary evidence. 
 145. It follows from the above, and the Board of Appeal  therefore concludes, that the documentary evidence 
concerning the representation of the legal entities in  this case did raise clear concerns and doubts, and 
was not sufficiently up-to-date or complete, to permit the CPVO to register the transfer.  
 146. As regards the documentation concer ning the transfer of the PVR itself, the Board of Appeal takes the 
view that it is important for the CPVO to ensure that the documentary evidence does not raise clear 
concerns and doubts regarding the tran sfer of the PVR. In this regard, the Board of Appeal notes, amongst 
other provisions, the requirement for “ official documents confirming the tran sfer, or of such extracts from 
those documents as suffice to establish the transfer ” set out at Article 79 of the IRR. Moreover, the Board 
of Appeal notes the CPVO regarding “ Assignments - Validity ”
9 which states: 
 
 
   
147. In this case, from the documentation made availabl e to the Board of Appeal, it appears that there are 
legitimate grounds supporting the view that the do cumentary evidence raises concerns and doubts 
regarding the transfer of the Queen PVR. That is because – and without it being necessary to assess this 
issue in detail given the conclusions already drawn ab ove – from the documentary evidence provided, the 
Board of Appeal notes that the wording contained in  the 3-paged document referred to at paragraph [5] 
above and concerning the transfer the Queen PVR could, on its face, be construed as insufficiently clear, precise and/or unambiguous to permit registration of a transfer.  The Board of Appeal notes in particular 
that some of the wording contained within the 3-pa ged document could, for example, be construed as 
transferring exploitation rights of  the Queen PVR, rather than the Qu een PVR itself. Without the Board of

     24 
 
Appeal in any way opining or taking a view on whet her or not the Queen PVR was transferred to SGI in 
this case, the Board of Appeal merely notes that, pu rsuant to the current rule s and guidance incumbent 
on the CPVO, the relevant wording in  the transfer document should be  sufficiently clear to enable the 
CPVO to register the transfer of a PVR. In the abse nce of documents which properly establish the existence 
of a transfer, there is no justification for the CPVO to  register a transfer (see, by analogy, Order of the 
General Court of 7 November 2023, Sattvica SA v European Union Inte llectual Property Office (EUIPO) , 
Case T-299/22, ECLI:EU:T:2023:710, paragraphs 39-43).       
 148. It follows from the above, and the Board of Appeal therefore concludes, that there are legitimate grounds 
supporting the view that the documentary evidence rais es concerns and doubts regarding the transfer of 
the Queen PVR and the wording in the documentary eviden ce could be construed as insufficient to permit 
the CPVO to register the transfer.  
 149. In light of the above, the Board of Appeal co ncludes that the documentary evidence in place was 
insufficient to permit the registration  of the transfer in this case.   
 150. In addition to the pleas and arguments summarised above, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has 
also brought forward arguments assert ing that, in this case, insofar as  the Appellant company ceased or 
withdrew its appointment of Mr Renán as a repres entative – the legal onus and burden was on the 
Appellant company to “ immediately ” inform the CPVO of this issue.  According to the CPVO, the CPVO 
cannot be held responsible for registering the transfer  of the PVR right due to a failure of the Appellant 
company to immediately inform the CPVO of the cess ation or withdrawal of Mr Renán’s permission to 
represent the Appellant company. The CPVO has referre d to Article 73(4) of the IRR which states that “ A 
procedural representative whose ma ndate has ended shall continue to  be considered as procedural 
representative until t he termination of his mandate has been communicate d to the Office
.” 
 
151. The Board of Appeal note s the following points.   
 152. Firstly, and as stated at para graph [143] above, from the document ary evidence made available to the 
Board of Appeal, it is not apparent that the A ppellant company has ever appointed Mr Renán as a 
representative before the CPVO as regards procedures  involving the registration  of the transfer of the 
Queen PVR to third parties. 
 153. Secondly, Article 73(4) of the IRR relates to ‘procedu ral representatives’ within the meaning of Article 82 
of the Basic Regulation. As is clear from Article 82 of  the Basic Regulation, ‘pro cedural representatives’ 
are a specific type of representative established by  the legislature which are capable of being appointed 
in specific situations. The legal concept of a ‘proce dural representative’ under Article 82 of the Basic 
Regulation may be comparable to, fo r example, ‘only representatives’ ap pointed pursuant to Article 8 of 
Regulation 1907/2006. 
 154. As such, the provisions concerning procedural repres entatives do not per se relate, and were not intended 
to relate, to all natural persons appointed to act on  behalf of a legal entity. Those provisions cannot 
de 
facto, and automatically apply to every situation invo lving the appointment of  a natural person to 
represent a legal person (e.g. a company) in proceedin gs involving the CPVO. Pursuant to the principles 
of good administration, legal certainty and legitimate expectation, it is genera lly for the CPVO to state 
clearly how the CPVO will take proportionate steps to  confirm natural persons have been appointed to 
represent a legal person in proceedings involving th e CPVO. Documents provided by the CPVO concerning 
the representation of legal persons before the CPVO – should be clear and precise, and should not lead 
to confusion vis-à-vis the appointm ent and obligations incumbent on ‘p rocedural representatives’.     
 
155. Thirdly, to the extent that the CPVO asserts the CP VO was right to consider Mr Renán as a representative 
of the Appellant company as regards the transfer of the Queen PVR, as the Appellant company failed to 
“immediately ” inform the CPVO that Mr Renán was no longer its representative – the Board of Appeal 
notes that this argument cannot su cceed. It is settled case-law that: “…Community legislation must be 
certain and its application foreseeable by individuals. T he principle of legal certainty requires that every 
measure of the instit utions having legal effects must be clear  and precise and must be brought to the 
notice of the person concerned in su ch a way that he can ascertain exac tly the time at which the measure 
comes into being and starts to have legal effects ” (emphasis added) (See the Judgment of the Court of 
First Instance of 22 January 1997, Opel Austria v Council , Case T-115/94 ECR II-39, ECLI:EU:T:1997:3, 
paragraph 124 and the case-law cited therein). If the CPVO  wishes to pursue a policy requiring titleholders 
to “immediately ” inform the CPVO of the cessation of an indivi dual’s legal powers of representation, it is 
necessary for the CPVO to inform stakeholders of this policy in accordance with the principles of good administration, legal certainty and legitimate expectat ion. The CPVO is therefore bound to bring this to 

     25 
 
the attention and notice of stakeholders, and the lega lity of such a policy, including its implementation, 
would be considered in light of, amongst other things , the principle of proportionality.   The CPVO has 
not brought forward any sufficient evidence that the CPVO has or is informing and warning stakeholders 
of this policy. The Board of Appeal can find no relevant reference in the guidance or in the current template fo rms,  advising o r warning s takeho lders to  “
immediately ” inform the CPVO if and when they cancel, 
withdraw, or revoke an individual’s ability to represent them.  
 156. In light of the above, the Board of Appeal conclude s that the CPVO’s arguments asserting that at the time 
it registered the transfer, the CPVO was right to a ssume Mr Renán acted as the valid representative of 
the Appellant company as regards the registra tion of the transfer – cannot succeed.   
 
157. On the basis of the initial conclusi ons and other points set out above, in  particular the applicability of the 
duty of diligence, and the inability for the CPVO’s arguments to succeed in this context, the Board of 
Appeal concludes that: the CPVO did not act in accordan ce with the duty of diligence, the CPVO failed to 
fully discharge its duty of diligence before registering the transfer, and, as such, the CPVO acted in breach 
of that duty. This constitutes a rele vant error in the appraisal of the fa cts and law, and a breach of a rule 
of law. The Board of Appeal therefore concludes that this plea is well founded and should be upheld.  
 158. Without it being necessary to assess the other pleas in law, the Board of Appeal  therefore concludes that 
the act of registering the transfer of  the Queen PVR from the Appellant to  SGI on the Register, is vitiated 
by a manifest error of EU law and must therefore be vacated.
